**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, by the dim glow of the oil lamp that flickers like a dying star in the night. The wind howls through the tower like a banshee, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the storm of my own soul. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves claw at the rocks below, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me like a shroud.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. When the world grew cold and the hours stretched into endless nights, it was your voice, your laughter, your presence that kept me from drowning in the loneliness of this lonely post. But now, as the storm gathers strength and I stand alone in this tower, I find myself wondering—what have I lost in the silence between us?

I have tried to write to you, many times, but the words always falter. I fear that what I have to say is too heavy, too raw, and that the truth would shatter the fragile peace you have built for yourself. Perhaps you have already learned what I have not: that some wounds do not heal, and some doors, once closed, can never be opened again.

You were young when you left, and I was too proud to say what I truly felt. I told myself that you needed to see the world beyond this tower, that you needed to

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not have the strength to say what I must.

You have not written to me in five years. Not a single letter. Not a word. I have waited, as I always have, for the day you would return to me, to see the light that guides the ships, to walk these cobbled paths and hear the lark sing in the morning. But you have not come. And I have not heard from you.

I know you think me cruel, that I have turned my back on you. But I have not. I have only turned my back on the world, and on the pain that comes with it. I have not spoken to you because I have not known how. I have not known what to say. You were my light, my hope, my only child. And now you are gone.

But tonight, as the storm gathers, I feel something I have not felt in a long time—fear. Not for myself, but for you. For the world you will one day inherit, and for the heart that may break if you do not find your way home. I fear I have not been the father you

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1897*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless, the sky is bruised with thunder, and I fear the storm may take more than just my life tonight. But if I do not, I must speak—before it is too late.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you told me I was not the man you needed, and I have not spoken a word to you since. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, but the fire in my heart has gone out. I have been alone in this place, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and I have wondered if you ever thought of me at all.

You were young then, full of anger and a heart that I could not understand. I tried to be the father you needed, but I failed. I failed to see the pain you carried, the silence you spoke in. I thought I was protecting you, but I only pushed you further away.

But tonight... tonight is different. I have seen the signs. The wind is rising, the sea is rising, and I do not know if I will make it through this. I have spent my life keeping the light for others, but now

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with the sea howling at my window and the wind tearing at the very fabric of this old lighthouse. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end with me still standing here, clutching the beam of this lamp like a lifeline.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not a single whisper of your voice. You vanished like the tide—sudden, silent, and cruel. I have tried to forgive, but forgiveness is not a thing that comes easily, especially when the heart is left to rot in the cold dark.

I know you think I have no right to write to you now. That you have no place in my life, and that I have no place in yours. But I am still here, as I have always been. I have not abandoned this lighthouse, nor have I abandoned the duty that binds me to this place. And though I have not been able to bind you to me, I still carry your name, still light the lamp, still watch over the sea that has taken so much from me.

You were my light, Eleanor. My only light. And now, as I face this storm, I wonder if I am not the only one left to carry it.

If this letter finds you, if you are still alive and still listening, I beg you

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**Whom I have not seen in five years, and with whom I have not spoken in five years, and who I have not written to in five years, and who I have not thought of in five years, save for the occasional flicker of the lighthouse light through the fog...**

I write this with the ink of the sea, the salt of the wind, and the weight of my own guilt pressing upon my chest like the storm that is coming. You would not believe how often I have looked up from the beacon, hoping to see your face in the distance, or your silhouette against the horizon. But you are not here. You have gone, as I have gone, and I have not spoken to you in five years, not even in the silence between the waves.

I have not spoken to you because I am afraid of what I might say. I am afraid of what I might feel. I am afraid of what you might think of me. You left with the wind, with the tide, with the promise of something greater than this little island, this little life, this little man who once loved you more than the sea.

But I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the way your hair caught the light when you stood at the edge of the cliffs, the way your voice echoed like a bell through the hollows of the rocks. I have not forgotten the way you looked at me, not with love

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with regret, and with the knowledge that perhaps this may be the last letter I ever send. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea rages with fury. I can feel the very bones of this old lighthouse trembling beneath me. Tonight, the storm will test us all.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not been the man I once was. Time has worn me down, as it does all things. I have long since ceased to be the father you once knew, and I fear that by the time you read this, I may not be here to see the sun rise again.

I have spent these years in solitude, tending to this lighthouse, watching the sea with a vigilance that has become second nature. I have seen many storms, but none like this. The waves are monstrous, crashing against the cliffs with a force that could shatter the very stone. I have not slept in days, and I fear that I may not live to see dawn.

I have thought often of you, Eleanor. Of the girl who once ran through the garden with a stick in her hand, pretending to be a pirate captain. I have thought of the way you would look at the sea from the window, wide-eyed and curious, as if it held some secret only you could decipher. You were always so full of life, so full of questions. I tried to

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast at the door, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very earth. The lighthouse stands strong, though I fear it may not stand much longer. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I will see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of nights spent staring at the sea, wondering if you had ever thought of me at all. I have not spoken to you, not even in the language of the waves, and I fear I may never again. But tonight, as the heavens darken and the gale grows fiercer, I must write.

You were a child when you ran away, Eliza. A child who believed the world would always be kind, that love was infinite and that no one could ever truly hurt you. You were wrong. I was wrong. I did not know how to hold you, how to make you feel safe. I did not know how to be a father. I thought if I gave you space, if I let you go, you would find your way back. But you did not. And I did not.

I have spent these years in regret, in quiet sorrow, in the belief that I had failed you. But now, as I face this storm, I wonder—what if I have failed not you, but myself? What if I have

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury I have not heard in many years. I write this with trembling hands, for I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must speak to you—though I fear the words may not be the ones I intend.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought me any closer to understanding the pain you must have felt. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to forgive. I have tried to believe that your silence was not a rejection of me, but of something greater—perhaps a need to find your own way in a world that has always been too harsh for you.

I have watched the storms that have passed, and I have stood at the edge of the cliff, listening to the waves crash against the rocks. Each time, I have thought of you, and each time, I have felt the weight of the years pressing down upon me. I have no one else to speak to, and so I write to you, though I know you may never read these words.

If you are reading this, know that I am not the man I once was. The loneliness has worn me down, and the silence of this place has made me forget how to speak. But I would

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow. Five long years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and though the sea has a way of keeping secrets, I have kept mine for far too long.

The wind howls through the cliffs like a wounded beast, and the waves, they grow ever more furious. I have lit the lamp this evening, as I have done for every storm since you left, but tonight, I feel the weight of the sea upon my bones. I do not know if the light will guide the ships, nor if it will guide me.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Your laughter, your bright eyes, the way you would run through the fog with your little dog, Bessie, and call me "Papa." I tried to keep that light alive, even when you were gone. But the years have dimmed it, and the storm has only made it worse.

I have not written to you, not once. I told myself that words would not mend what was broken, that silence was the only way to bear the ache. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the wind screams like a banshee, I find myself writing, hoping that if the sea takes me, you might read this and know that I did not forget you.

I have always been a man of the sea, Eleanor. I have known its moods, its

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not only from the cold but from the weight of words that have long lain dormant within me. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the sea has not forgotten me, I fear I have forgotten how to speak to you.

The storm that now gathers on the horizon is no mere squall—it is a beast of the elements, and I have seen many such in my time. The lighthouse stands as it always has, a sentinel against the tempest, but I wonder if it will hold when the winds howl like wolves and the waves rise like mountains. I have not slept in days, and the sea has been restless, as if it knows the end of my time is near.

You were always the child of the sea, Eleanor. I remember the day you first stood on the cliff, eyes wide with wonder, as if the world was yours to explore. You had such a fire in you, a spirit that could not be tamed. But that same fire burned too hot, and in time, it turned to embers. We grew apart, and though I have not forgotten the days we spent together, I have not spoken of them since.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and in that time, I have seen many storms. Each one a reminder of the fragility of life. I have watched men brave and bold fall to the sea, and

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the days have dragged on like the endless hours of the night, I find myself haunted by the silence between us. Tonight, a storm is gathering on the horizon, one that promises to test the very limits of this old lighthouse and the strength of my resolve. I fear I may not see dawn, and if I do not, I hope you will find this letter in the morning.

You were but a child when you left, and I, in my foolish pride, allowed my anger to cloud my judgment. I believed that by keeping my distance, I was protecting you from the harsh realities of life. But I was wrong. You were not a child to be sheltered from the world, nor was I a man who could so easily sever the bond between father and daughter.

I have spent these years watching the sea, its fury a mirror to my own soul. Each wave that crashes against the rocks is a reminder of the love I have lost and the words I have not spoken. I have not been the same since you left, and I fear I may never be again. The light of the lighthouse has grown dim in my eyes, much like the light of our relationship.

If I do not survive this storm, I beg you, do not let the bitterness of our past cloud your heart. Remember the girl who once

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same state of silence that has kept us apart for these five long years. I have written many letters, Eliza, but none have ever found their way to you. Perhaps they were lost in the sea, or perhaps they were never sent. I do not know, and I do not care. What matters is that I write now, with the storm gathering on the horizon and the wind howling like a wounded beast.

You were always the clever one, my girl. I remember the day you first climbed the tower, no older than ten, with your little notebook and your dreams of the stars. You had such a way with words, such a way with people. I used to think you would grow up to be a writer, or a poet, or perhaps a teacher. But you chose something else, and I do not know what it was. I only know that you left, and I did not stop you.

I have been here, alone, with the sea and the wind and the lamp. I have kept the light burning, as I always have. I have told myself that it is my duty, and that I must not falter. But sometimes, when the storm is fierce and the waves crash like thunder, I wonder if I am doing this for the world, or for you.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast through the cracks in the stone, and I feel the weight of the world pressing down on my old bones. The sea, that endless, restless thing, roars against the cliffs with a fury that seems almost human. It is as if the very earth is weeping. And in this moment, I find myself thinking of you—of the last time I saw you, and the last words we spoke.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us feels longer still. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried to write, to reach out, to explain. But every letter I have penned has been lost in the post, or returned unanswered. You have not answered a single one. And I wonder, with a bitter ache in my chest, if you ever truly heard them.

You were always so proud, always so certain of your own path. I remember the day you stood at the door, your eyes sharp with defiance, your voice trembling with something I could not name—was it anger? Or sorrow? I do not know. You said you could not stay here, that the life I led was not the life you wanted. And I let you go. I did not stop you. I did not even try to hold you back.

But now, as the storm gathers, as the sea rises and the sky turns

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart that feels as though it might shatter beneath the weight of what I must say. It is late, and the wind howls like a beast in pain, rattling the shutters of this old lighthouse as though it would tear it down. I have not seen the sea in such a fury since I first took up this duty, and I fear that tonight may be the last night I ever see the sky.

You have not written to me in five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years. I have kept the light burning for the sailors, but I have not kept the light burning for you. I have not spoken to you, not in words, not in letters, not even in my thoughts. And yet, I have thought of you more than I can bear to admit.

I know you think me cruel, that I have abandoned you. But I did not. I have never stopped thinking of you. I have not forgotten the sound of your laughter, the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of the sea, or the way you would wrap your arms around me when you were little and the storm would roar outside. I have kept that memory alive, even when the world outside this tower has seemed to fall apart.

You were never meant to grow up without a father. I know that. I know that I have failed you, and I know that you must hate me.

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*Dearest Eleanor,*

I write this with the storm howling at my back, the sea roaring like a beast that will not be tamed. The wind whips through the lighthouse’s narrow windows, and the waves crash against the rocks below like a mournful chorus. I’ve stood here for over twenty years, guiding ships through the dark, and tonight, I feel the weight of the world on my shoulders. But it’s not the sea that troubles me—it’s the silence of your absence.

Five years have passed since you left. I’ve tried to forget, to let time mend what words could not. But the memory lingers like the scent of salt in the air, sharp and persistent. I’ve seen your name in the ledger, in the letters I never sent, in the moments I’ve imagined you sitting by the fire, reading by candlelight. I’ve tried to be a father, but I’ve failed you. I’ve tried to be a man, but I’ve failed you.

The storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn. The light has been failing for weeks, and the generator is on its last breath. I’ve worked through the night, oiling the lamp, checking the lenses, praying that the beacon will hold. But I fear it will not. And if it fails, if I fail, I want you to know

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and though the days have dragged on like the slow turning of the lighthouse beam, I have endured. I have kept the light burning for you, for all who sail these waters, and for the memory of the daughter I once had.

You were not the first to leave, nor shall you be the last. But you were the first to take your silence with you, and the last to leave your mark on my soul.

I do not write this letter to beg for your return, for I have long since ceased to beg. I write it because the storm is coming—dark, unrelenting, and full of fury. The sea is rising, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. I have no one to call for help, no one to mourn my passing, save perhaps the sea itself, which has always known my name.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Not the fire that burns in the tower, but the warmth that kept me from drowning in the cold of loneliness. You were my reason for rising each morning, for enduring the endless hours of duty, for believing that perhaps, somewhere in the world, there was still good in men and women. You were my hope.

And now, that hope is gone.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink trembling in my hand, as if the very act of writing might summon the storm that now churns beyond the cliffs. The sea has been restless all day, and I have watched the waves rise like the ghosts of my own regrets. You would not believe the things I have seen tonight—how the sky has turned the color of old blood, how the wind howls like a beast that has been denied its prey.

Five years. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken to you, not even in the letters I have sent, which you have never answered. I suppose you have no reason to believe they ever reached you. Perhaps they were lost in the storm, or perhaps you have chosen to ignore them. I do not blame you. I would not blame you either.

But tonight, as the tempest approaches, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever. I have spent my life watching the sea, guiding the ships, keeping the light burning. And yet, I have failed to keep the light of our love alive. I have failed you, Eleanor. I have failed you in every way that matters.

You were a child when you left, and I did not stop you. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world was too cruel, that the sea was too harsh. But I was wrong. I was wrong to let you go. I was wrong to let the

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now, not with the hope that you will read these words, but with the knowledge that they may be the last ones I ever put to paper. The sea is rising, the winds howl like the very devil, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. I have kept this lighthouse for over thirty years, and I have seen many storms. But this one... this one feels different.

I have not written to you in five years, and I do not know if you will ever forgive me. You were so young when you left, and I was not the father you needed. I was too caught up in the duty of this place, too consumed by the sea. I told myself that I was protecting you, that I was doing what was right. But in truth, I was afraid. Afraid of what it would mean to let you go, afraid of what it would mean to lose you.

You were the light in my life, Eleanor. The one thing that made the endless nights bearable. I remember the day you came to visit, when you were just a child. You ran up the steps, your little hands gripping the railing, and you looked at me with such wonder. "Daddy, the sea is so big," you said. I told you it was beautiful, that it was a place of power and mystery, and that I would always protect you.

But I failed you. I failed you

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea roars in protest, and I can only hope that the storm that rages tonight will not claim me before I can reach you. Five years have passed since you left, and though I have tried to live each day with the hope that you might return, I fear I have been foolish to believe in a reunion. Yet I still hold on to that hope, even as the world around me crumbles.

I have not written to you in so long, and I do not know if you will ever read these words. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves rise like a vengeful titan, I feel the weight of my silence pressing down on me. I have kept so much inside, and now I must release it.

I have always loved you, Eliza. Not as a father should, perhaps, but with the deepest of hearts. I have watched you grow, and I have seen the pain you carried, the burden you bore. I know now that I was not the man you needed, and I will never be the man you deserve. But I have never stopped loving you, and I never will.

I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, watching the sea, the stars, and the endless horizon. I have found peace in the rhythm of the waves, in the steady

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you with a heart heavy as the storm that gathers beyond these cliffs, its winds howling like the ghosts of those I have lost. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has worn away the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the bitterness that lingers in my soul.

I have spent these years in solitude, the lighthouse my only companion, its beam a silent witness to my sorrow. I have watched the sea rage and the skies darken, and each night, I have wondered if I would ever see your face again. Yet, I have not written. I have not reached out. And now, as the tempest approaches, I find myself questioning whether it is too late to mend what has been broken.

The wind has grown fierce, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that mirrors the storm within me. I have always believed in duty, in steadfastness, in the quiet strength of a man who does not falter. But tonight, as I stand before the light, I feel the weight of years pressing upon me, and I am not sure I will survive this night.

If I do not, I hope you will know that I have not forgotten you. That I have not ceased to love you. And that, in the end, I would have given anything to have you back in my life.

With all my heart,  
Your Father

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 12th, 1887*

My Dearest Eliza,

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and the days have worn upon me like the relentless tide. The sea here is restless, and the wind howls through the cliffs as though the very earth mourns the silence between us. I have not spoken your name in all this time, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers and the darkness creeps closer, I find myself reaching for the quill, as if the words might carry through the gale and find you.

You were fifteen when you left, a girl with fire in her eyes and a heart full of dreams. I tried to stop you, but you were like the wind—unstoppable. You spoke of a life beyond these rocks, beyond the salt and the sea, and I could not bring myself to deny you. I told myself it was for your own good, that you would find your place in the world, but I have not seen you since.

The lighthouse stands as it always has, though the light has grown dimmer of late. The lantern is old, and the gears are worn. I have tended to it with the same care I have given to my duty, but I fear that this storm may be the end of it. I have heard the sea roar like a beast in pain, and the waves have cre

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for many a stormy night, and I pray that its light will guide some lost soul home, even as it may not guide me back to you.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have you written to me. I do not know if you still remember the days when we were close, or if time has turned you away from the family that once held you dear. I have not heard from you in the years since your mother passed, and I have not been able to bear the silence that has followed. I have tried to reach you, but words have always failed me, and I have feared that even if I did speak, you would not listen.

Tonight, however, something has changed. The storm is unlike any I have seen in my years upon this rock. The waves rise like mountains, and the wind is a living thing, howling through the cracks of this old tower. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I know when it is not merely storming, but raging with fury. I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. I have prepared my will, and I have made my peace

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health and happier circumstances than I have been in these past five years. Time has a cruel way of passing, and I find myself wondering if you still remember the days when we were a family, when the sea was not a threat but a companion, and the lighthouse was not a prison but a home.

You were always a spirited child, full of questions and curiosity. I remember how you used to climb the stairs to the lantern room, eager to see the world from above. Those were the days when I believed that no storm could ever break us apart. But time, like the sea, is relentless, and it has worn down both our hearts.

Tonight, a storm is gathering on the horizon, one that threatens to swallow the lighthouse whole. The winds howl like wolves, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have never seen. I fear that I may not see the dawn. But even in my darkest hour, I find solace in the thought that you are out there, somewhere, living your life, perhaps unaware of the weight I carry.

I have not written to you in years, and I know that words may not be enough to mend what has been broken. But I write this not as a man who has lost hope, but as a father who still longs for the day when we may reconcile. If there is still a chance, if you are willing to listen

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in sorrow, for I have not written to you in five long years. Time, it seems, has not been kind to our bond, and I am left to wonder if you still remember the man I once was—or if you have forgotten me entirely.

I write to you now not from the comfort of my study, but from the dim light of the lighthouse, where I have spent the better part of my life. The sea, you see, is a fickle mistress, and tonight, it is restless. A storm brews on the horizon, dark as a storm cloud on a summer’s day, and I fear it may not pass without leaving its mark upon us all.

You were always the child who loved the sea. I remember the way you would sit on the rocks, watching the waves crash against the cliffs, your eyes wide with wonder. You would ask me questions that no one else ever did—why the sea sings, why the stars shine so brightly at night, and why the lighthouse always shines, even when no one is watching. I used to answer you with stories, but now I wonder if you still remember them.

I have not seen you in five years, Eleanor. Five years is a long time to be apart from one's child. I have tried to write, but the words would not come. Perhaps I was afraid of what I might

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of silence, for I fear the storm that now rages beyond these stone walls may be the last I see of this world—and of you. The wind howls like a wounded beast, clawing at the windows, and the sea roars as if it too has a voice, a memory, a curse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the sky cracks with lightning and the waves crash like the fists of the gods, I feel the truth of my guilt more than ever.

You were right to walk away. I was wrong to let pride and stubbornness blind me to the pain I caused. I thought I could keep the light burning for you, even when my own heart had long gone dark. But you were never the one I was trying to protect. You were the one I failed to understand.

I know you think I never loved you, that I was too distant, too cold, too wrapped up in the duty of this lighthouse. But I was not cruel. I was broken. I carried the weight of this place like a cross, and in my loneliness, I forgot how to speak, how to reach for you. I thought the sea would keep my secrets, and the silence would keep you from knowing the truth of what I was.

You were the light I always wished to shine for, and I let it go.

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls outside like the very devil himself is vying for the soul of this lighthouse, and I feel as though I am being torn apart by the storm within me—just as I have been for the past five years.

It has been five long, bitter years since I last saw you, and five years since I last heard your voice. I have not written, nor have I dared to speak, for fear that the words might not carry the truth, or worse, that they might be too much for you to bear. But tonight, the storm has come, and with it, a strange sense of reckoning. I must write, Eliza. I must try.

**To My Dearest, My Only, My Lost Child,**

I write this with trembling hands, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside these walls. The wind is rising, the waves are mounting, and I fear I may not see another dawn. But if I do not, I want you to know—before the storm swallows me whole—that I have not forgotten you. Not truly. Not in the way you think.

But I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid that if I showed you my weakness, you would leave me again.
